region_of_origin: South India (Tamil Nadu); widely adopted in Singapore
food_subgroup: Deep-fried crispy spiral snack made from rice flour and urad dal flour with spices

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for murukku or South Indian deep-fried rice-lentil spiral snacks. No defensible exact URI available; closest parent would be a generic fried snack food concept.
ontology.foodex2_description: Snacks (savoury) — closest parent category for deep-fried cereal-legume snack products; no specific murukku code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for murukku. A03XK (savoury snacks) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for murukku or equivalent South Indian fried snack.

serving.natural_language_portion: One realistic serving of murukku is approximately 30g (1 handful or 3 medium spirals), providing about 148 kcal, 7.5g fat, 18g carbohydrate, and 2.7g protein.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Murukku (deep-fried rice flour and urad dal snack)
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods is an approved primary source for Indian dishes. Murukku (rice flour and urad dal) is a standard entry in this database. Values for monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and water_g are not disaggregated in the ICMR-NIN entry and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Rice Flour and Legume-Based Snacks — analogous estimate based on rice flour snack GI data
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate); supported by peer-reviewed literature on rice flour and urad dal GI values
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for murukku exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of ~65 (medium) is estimated from: (1) rice flour-based snacks typically GI 65–72 in the Sydney GI Database; (2) urad dal (black gram) has GI ~43, which partially moderates the overall GI of the composite snack; (3) deep-frying reduces starch gelatinisation and lowers GI relative to baked or boiled rice products. The composite estimate of GI ~65 is consistent with similar extruded rice-legume snacks in the literature. GL per 30g serving = (65 × 18g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 11.7.

hypertension.guidance: A standard 30g serving contributes 105mg sodium (7% of the 1500mg daily target for hypertensive patients), which is manageable in isolation, but Deepavali consumption of 100–150g of murukku alone delivers 350–525mg sodium — clinicians should counsel patients to limit festival snack portions and account for cumulative sodium from all festival foods consumed in a single day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (~65), moderate glycemic load (GL 11.7 per 30g); high energy density; festival overconsumption risk
type2_diabetes.guidance: A single 30g serving (GL 11.7) is within acceptable limits for T2DM patients, but the realistic Deepavali consumption pattern of 100–150g raises GL to 39–59 from murukku alone — patients with T2DM should be advised to strictly limit murukku to one 30g portion per occasion and monitor post-prandial glucose during festival periods.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High energy density (493 kcal/100g), fat content 25g/100g; saturated fat 4g/100g; zero cholesterol; primarily PUFA/MUFA from vegetable frying oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Murukku is energy-dense and moderately high in total fat, but saturated fat is relatively low at 4g/100g (1.2g per 30g serving) and cholesterol is zero; patients with CVD should limit portions to 30g and avoid frequent consumption given the high caloric density and cumulative fat load during festival eating occasions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, naturally gluten-free, moderate iron content
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Murukku is naturally gluten-free (relevant for RA patients with associated coeliac disease or gluten sensitivity), low in saturated fat, and provides 2.0mg iron/100g; a 30g serving is generally well-tolerated for RA patients, though deep-fried foods should be consumed in moderation as part of an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Murukku (Tamil: முறுக்கு, meaning 'twisted' or 'crooked') is the quintessential South Indian deep-fried spiral snack, made from a dough of rice flour and urad dal (black gram) flour seasoned with cumin seeds, sesame seeds, asafoetida, and salt. The dough is pressed through a star-shaped mould directly into hot oil and fried until golden-brown and hard-crunchy. The result is a savoury, slightly nutty, cumin-sesame flavoured snack that is naturally gluten-free and vegan. In Singapore, murukku is a staple of the Tamil community and is particularly prominent during Deepavali (Diwali), when it is gifted and consumed in large quantities across home visits. It is widely available at Mustafa Centre, Little India shops, and major supermarkets. Nutritionally, murukku is energy-dense at 493 kcal/100g with 25g fat and 60g carbohydrate per 100g. A realistic reference serving of 30g (1 handful or 3 medium spirals) provides approximately 148 kcal, 7.5g fat, 18g carbohydrate, 2.7g protein, and GL of 11.7. The GI is estimated at ~65 (medium), moderated by the urad dal component relative to pure rice flour snacks. The critical clinical issue is festival overconsumption: patients may consume 100–200g across a day of Deepavali home visits, raising GL to 39–78 and sodium to 350–700mg from murukku alone. Cholesterol is zero and saturated fat is relatively low (4g/100g), making the fat profile more favourable than many fried snacks. Murukku is also known as chakli in Maharashtra and Karnataka, and as janthikalu or chegodilu in Andhra Pradesh and Telangana.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, which is an approved primary source for Indian dishes and provides a direct entry for murukku (rice flour and urad dal deep-fried snack). GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for rice flour snacks and peer-reviewed urad dal GI literature, clearly documented as an estimate.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid disaggregation for murukku is not reported in the ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods database; the total fat value (25g/100g) is confirmed but MUFA/PUFA breakdown is unavailable from this approved source. water_g set to -1: moisture content for murukku is not reported in the ICMR-NIN entry consulted. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN; traditional vegetable oil frying does not produce significant trans fats). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — murukku is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients when made with vegetable oil). vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_d_mcg, and vitamin_b12_mcg are 0.0 (naturally zero or below detection threshold for this plant-based fried snack; confirmed by ICMR-NIN). GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for rice flour-based snacks and peer-reviewed literature on urad dal GI; no direct GI measurement for murukku exists in the Sydney database. Dietary flag 'vegan' applies when murukku is made with vegetable oil rather than butter/ghee; the traditional recipe may use butter. Allergen: sesame seeds are a primary ingredient and a declared allergen. FoodEx2 code A03XK assigned as closest parent (savoury snacks); no specific murukku code exists.
